author: Irenaeus of Lyon
interpretation: Irenaeus understood the New Jerusalem as a literal, physical city that would descend from heaven to a renewed earth during the millennial kingdom. He insisted on the material reality of the resurrection and the renewed creation against Gnostics who spiritualized the body and the physical world. The dimensions of the city, the river of life, and the tree of life are all real features of the coming kingdom. God's purpose from creation was always to dwell physically with humanity in a glorified material world.
context: Irenaeus's literalist, materialist reading was rooted in his anti-Gnostic theology: if matter is evil, as the Gnostics claimed, then a physical new creation makes no sense. His defense of bodily resurrection and physical new creation shaped the church's creedal affirmation of the resurrection of the body.

author: Augustine of Hippo
interpretation: Augustine read the New Jerusalem as the final glorified state of the City of God — the community of the redeemed in their eternal perfection. While affirming bodily resurrection and a real new creation, he emphasized that the city's symbolic dimensions (the perfect cube echoing the Holy of Holies, the twelve gates for the twelve tribes, the jeweled foundations for the twelve apostles) convey theological meaning about the perfection and completeness of God's people. The absence of a temple means that God himself is the temple — the entire new creation is holy space.
context: Augustine's reading established the theological framework for understanding Revelation 21–22 as both literally real and symbolically rich, avoiding the extremes of pure literalism and pure allegory. His emphasis on the absence of the temple as the climax of salvation history has been deeply influential.

author: Bernard of Clairvaux
source_title: Sermons on the Song of Songs; De Diligendo Deo
interpretation: Bernard interpreted the New Jerusalem through the lens of mystical union with God. The city represents the soul's final arrival at perfect contemplation of the divine, where the beatific vision replaces all earthly knowing. The river of life is the continuous flow of divine love; the tree of life is Christ himself, eternally nourishing the blessed. Bernard emphasized that the New Jerusalem's 'no more tears' is not merely the removal of suffering but the attainment of a joy so complete that even the memory of sorrow is swallowed up in glory.
context: Bernard's mystical reading influenced the entire medieval tradition of contemplative theology, in which the New Jerusalem became the ultimate metaphor for the soul's journey to God — accessible in partial experience through prayer and finally in full through death and resurrection.

author: John Calvin
source_title: Institutes of the Christian Religion (III.25); Psychopannychia
interpretation: Calvin did not write a commentary on Revelation but addressed the new creation in his Institutes and other writings. He affirmed a real, renewed physical creation but warned against speculative curiosity about its details. The key point is God's dwelling with humanity — the reversal of the fall's separation. Calvin stressed that the new creation's glory exceeds anything we can imagine from the symbolic language, and that believers should focus on the certainty of the hope rather than decoding the imagery.
context: Calvin's restraint about eschatological speculation while affirming the reality of the new creation became characteristic of Reformed theology's approach: confident hope in the substance, careful modesty about the details.

author: Jürgen Moltmann
source_title: Theology of Hope; The Coming of God
interpretation: Moltmann built his entire theological project around the eschatological promise of Revelation 21–22. The New Jerusalem is not an escape from this world but its transformation — God makes 'all things new,' not 'all new things.' The vision critiques every present political and economic order by revealing what they fall short of, and it energizes Christians to work toward justice, peace, and ecological renewal as anticipations of the coming city. The descent of the city from heaven means that the new creation is God's act, not human achievement, but it arrives into history, not away from it.
context: Moltmann's reading transformed the New Jerusalem from a purely future hope into a catalyst for present Christian engagement with the world, profoundly influencing liberation theology, ecological theology, and the social ethics of hope.

author: Richard Bauckham
source_title: The Theology of the Book of Revelation
interpretation: Bauckham argues that the New Jerusalem is John's most theologically dense vision, designed to reverse every dimension of the fall: the garden of Eden is restored (tree of life, river), the curse is lifted, and God's unmediated presence replaces the temple. The city is simultaneously a bride (relational), a city (communal), and a temple-garden (cosmic) — collapsing distinctions between human intimacy with God, social life, and creation's flourishing. The open gates and the 'healing of the nations' envision a universal scope of redemption that transcends ethnic and national boundaries.
context: Bauckham's integrative reading — combining Eden imagery, temple theology, and political critique — has become one of the most widely cited contemporary interpretations, showing how Revelation's final vision synthesizes the entire biblical narrative.
